This operation included the attack on Mers-el-Kébir.

Following the armistice between France and Germany in June 1940, the British government, fearing the French fleet would fall into Axis hands, initiated Operation Catapult. Vice Admiral Sir James Somerville commanded Force H, which was tasked with preventing this.
[1](https://historynet.com/operation-catapult-naval-destruction-at-mers-el-kebir/), [2](https://bmmhs.org/mers-el-kebir-sinking-the-french-fleet/), [3](https://warfarehistorynetwork.com/why-did-the-royal-navy-sink-the-french-fleet-in-world-war-ii/)
At Mers-el-Kébir, Algeria, the French fleet included modern battleships like *Dunkerque* and *Strasbourg*, older battleships such as *Bretagne* and *Provence*, the seaplane tender *Commandant Teste*, and several destroyers. [4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attack_on_Mers-el-K%C3%A9bir), [3](https://warfarehistorynetwork.com/why-did-the-royal-navy-sink-the-french-fleet-in-world-war-ii/)Somerville's forces presented an ultimatum to the French commander, Admiral Marcel-Bruno Gensoul, offering various options including joining the British, decommissioning the ships, or scuttling them. [1](https://historynet.com/operation-catapult-naval-destruction-at-mers-el-kebir/), [2](https://bmmhs.org/mers-el-kebir-sinking-the-french-fleet/), [3](https://warfarehistorynetwork.com/why-did-the-royal-navy-sink-the-french-fleet-in-world-war-ii/)When the French refused the terms, Force H opened fire.

[1](https://historynet.com/operation-catapult-naval-destruction-at-mers-el-kebir/)The attack resulted in the disablement or sinking of numerous French vessels, preventing them from being used by the Axis powers. While not all ships were "captured" in the traditional sense, the operation effectively neutralized a French fleet that varied considerably in size and type. A[2](https://bmmhs.org/mers-el-kebir-sinking-the-french-fleet/), [3](https://warfarehistorynetwork.com/why-did-the-royal-navy-sink-the-french-fleet-in-world-war-ii/)dditionally, as part of Operation Catapult, French ships in British ports like Plymouth and Portsmouth, including submarines, battleships, and destroyers, were boarded and captured by Royal Navy sailors.[2](https://bmmhs.org/mers-el-kebir-sinking-the-french-fleet/)']}}

result:
Vice Admiral Sir James Somerville was the commander who captured or neutralized a significant portion of the French fleet, comprising ships of varying sizes, during Operation Catapult in July 1940. This operation included the attack on Mers-el-Kébir.

Following the armistice between France and Germany in June 1940, the British government, fearing the French fleet would fall into Axis hands, initiated Operation Catapult. Vice Admiral Sir James Somerville commanded Force H, which was tasked with preventing this.
[1](https://historynet.com/operation-catapult-naval-destruction-at-mers-el-kebir/), [2](https://bmmhs.org/mers-el-kebir-sinking-the-french-fleet/), [3](https://warfarehistorynetwork.com/why-did-the-royal-navy-sink-the-french-fleet-in-world-war-ii/)
At Mers-el-Kébir, Algeria, the French fleet included modern battleships like *Dunkerque* and *Strasbourg*, older battleships such as *Bretagne* and *Provence*, the seaplane tender *Commandant Teste*, and several destroyers. [4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attack_on_Mers-el-K%C3%A9bir), [3](https://warfarehistorynetwork.com/why-did-the-royal-navy-sink-the-french-fleet-in-world-war-ii/)Somerville's forces presented an ultimatum to the French commander, Admiral Marcel-Bruno Gensoul, offering various options including joining the British, decommissioning the ships, or scuttling them. [1](https://historynet.com/operation-catapult-naval-destruction-at-mers-el-kebir/), [2](https://bmmhs.org/mers-el-kebir-sinking-the-french-fleet/), [3](https://warfarehistorynetwork.com/why-did-the-royal-navy-sink-the-french-fleet-in-world-war-ii/)When the French refused the terms, Force H opened fire.

[1](https://historynet.com/operation-catapult-naval-destruction-at-mers-el-kebir/)The attack resulted in the disablement or sinking of numerous French vessels, preventing them from being used by the Axis powers. While not all ships were "captured" in the traditional sense, the operation effectively neutralized a French fleet that varied considerably in size and type. A[2](https://bmmhs.org/mers-el-kebir-sinking-the-french-fleet/), [3](https://warfarehistorynetwork.com/why-did-the-royal-navy-sink-the-french-fleet-in-world-war-ii/)dditionally, as part of Operation Catapult, French ships in British ports like Plymouth and Portsmouth, including submarines, battleships, and destroyers, were boarded and captured by Royal Navy sailors.[2](https://bmmhs.org/mers-el-kebir-sinking-the-french-fleet/)

**Process Summary:** 
1. Initial analysis using Wikipedia revealed no direct information on a commander who captured all French ships of varying sizes. 
2. A Google search provided more detailed information, specifically pointing to Vice Admiral Sir James Somerville. He led the British naval operation (Operation Catapult) that targeted the French fleet in July 1940, neutralizing ships of various types and sizes at Mers-el-Kébir, Algeria, and in British ports. The French fleet included modern battleships, older battleships, seaplane tenders, and destroyers.
